skill.name: qiskit
skill.description: Qiskit guidance for building, transpiling, running, and visualizing quantum circuits on simulators and IBM Quantum hardware.
skill.summary: Build and run IBM Quantum workflows with Qiskit.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found Markdown documentation and Qiskit examples rather than malicious behavior. Shell blocks, quantum algorithm names, backend status calls, URLs, and file-save examples are legitimate for this skill. The remaining risk is low because users may run install commands, configure IBM Quantum tokens, access cloud services, and save local output files.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Blocks Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged backticks are Markdown fences around bash and Python examples. The skill does not execute these commands automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line context shows documentation fences and user-facing examples, not Ruby backtick execution. No evidence found of autonomous command execution.

low_findings.title: Quantum Terms Flagged as Malware or Weak Crypto
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Terms such as execute, backend, CNOT, QFT, and Shor's Algorithm are standard Qiskit concepts. They are not command-and-control logic or weak cryptography usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding text describes quantum circuit composition, primitives, and algorithm libraries. No evidence found of malware control flow or cryptographic implementation risk.

low_findings.title: Cloud Credential Examples Require User Care
low_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE as a low risk factor. The skill documents IBM Quantum token setup and cloud backend use. This is legitimate, but users should protect tokens and understand cloud execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token examples use placeholders and official Qiskit APIs, so malicious intent is not indicated. The risk is still real because users may store credentials locally.

low_findings.title: Local File Output Examples Are Benign
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for malicious filesystem activity. The examples save circuit figures, LaTeX diagrams, and VQE results to local files for normal research workflows.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file paths are fixed local output names in examples. No evidence found of reading secrets, overwriting sensitive paths, or exfiltrating files.

content.user_title: Build IBM Quantum Workflows with Qiskit
content.value_statement: Quantum workflows require accurate circuit construction, backend selection, and result interpretation. This skill guides Qiskit setup, execution, optimization, and visualization for simulators and IBM Quantum hardware.
actual_capabilities: Create Qiskit circuits with gates, measurements, barriers, and parameterized operations.
Run circuits with local Sampler and Estimator primitives for simulation workflows.
Configure IBM Quantum Runtime and choose cloud backends for hardware execution.
Transpile circuits for backend topology, native gates, and optimization levels.
Visualize circuits, histograms, backend maps, and quantum state representations.
Apply documented workflows for VQE, QAOA, Grover search, and quantum kernels.
limitations: Hardware execution requires a valid IBM Quantum account and network access.
The skill provides guidance and examples; it does not guarantee quantum speedups.
Real devices have queue delays, limited availability, and hardware noise.
Users must manage API tokens and cloud costs outside the skill.

use_cases.title: Prototype Quantum Circuits
use_cases.description: Build Bell states, GHZ states, parameterized circuits, and measurement workflows before moving to hardware.

use_cases.title: Prepare Hardware Runs
use_cases.description: Select IBM Quantum backends, transpile circuits, submit Runtime jobs, and retrieve results.

use_cases.title: Explore Quantum Algorithms
use_cases.description: Use VQE, QAOA, Grover search, and quantum kernel examples for optimization, chemistry, and machine learning studies.

prompt_templates.title: Create a First Circuit
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a two-qubit Bell state in Qiskit, measure both qubits, and explain the expected simulator result.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Local Simulation
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to run my Qiskit circuit with StatevectorSampler and summarize the measurement counts.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize for Hardware
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Qiskit circuit and explain how to transpile it for an IBM Quantum backend with optimization level three.

prompt_templates.title: Design a VQE Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a Qiskit VQE workflow using Runtime sessions, an ansatz, an observable, and convergence tracking.

output_examples.input: Create and simulate a three-qubit GHZ circuit.
output: A circuit plan that applies one Hadamard gate and two controlled-NOT gates.
Expected measurements concentrated on all-zero and all-one bitstrings.
A short explanation of why the result indicates multipartite entanglement.

output_examples.input: Prepare my circuit for IBM Quantum hardware.
output: A backend selection checklist covering status, qubit count, topology, and queue depth.
A transpilation plan that considers native gates and optimization level.
Execution notes for Runtime primitives, job IDs, and result retrieval.

output_examples.input: Explain when to use Sampler or Estimator.
output_examples.output: Use Sampler for bitstring distributions and sampling tasks. Use Estimator for expectation values, energies, and variational algorithms.

best_practices: Validate circuits with local simulators before submitting jobs to real hardware.
Transpile for the selected backend before hardware execution.
Store IBM Quantum tokens securely and avoid sharing notebooks with embedded secrets.
anti_patterns: Submitting untested circuits directly to paid or queued quantum hardware.
Using Sampler for expectation values or Estimator for count distributions.
Ignoring backend topology, noise, queue status, and shot budget during experiment design.

faq.question: Do I need an IBM Quantum account?
faq.answer: Local simulators do not require an account. Real IBM Quantum hardware requires an account and API token.

faq.question: Can this skill run quantum jobs by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples that users can run in their own environment.

faq.question: What is transpilation in Qiskit?
faq.answer: Transpilation adapts a circuit to a target backend, including topology, native gates, and optimization passes.

faq.question: When should I use Sampler?
faq.answer: Use Sampler when you need measurement bitstrings, counts, or probability distributions from circuits.

faq.question: When should I use Estimator?
faq.answer: Use Estimator when you need expectation values for observables, energies, or variational algorithms.

faq.question: Does this skill cover quantum algorithms?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for VQE, QAOA, Grover search, quantum kernels, and related Qiskit libraries.
